naval\\germany.json:
[
  {
    "id": "bismarck",
    "name": "Battleship Bismarck",
    "type": "Battleship",
    "nation": "Germany",
    "class": "Bismarck-class",
    "image": "/storage/images/armaments/naval/bismarck.jpg",
    "commissioned": "1940-08-24",
    "fate": "Sunk 27 May 1941",
    "displacement_tonnes": 50300,
    "length_m": 251,
    "crew": 2092,
    "armament": {
      "main": "8× 38 cm SK C/34 guns (4 twin turrets)",
      "secondary": ["12× 15 cm guns", "16× 10.5 cm guns", "16× 3.7 cm AA guns"],
      "aircraft": "4× Arado Ar 196 floatplanes"
    },
    "speed_knots": 30.1,
    "armor_mm": { "belt": 320, "deck": 80, "turret_face": 360 },
    "summary": "The Bismarck was the largest warship Germany had ever built. During her only operational sortie (Operation Rheinübung, May 1941), she sank the battlecruiser HMS Hood — Britain's most famous warship — with the loss of all but 3 of her 1,418 crew. She was sunk three days later after being hunted down by the Royal Navy.",
    "sources": [
      { "ref": "Kennedy, Ludovic. Pursuit: The Chase and Sinking of the Bismarck. Collins, 1974." },
      { "ref": "Ballard, Robert. The Discovery of the Bismarck. Hodder and Stoughton, 1990." }
    ],
    "related_records": [
      { "id": "rheinubung", "title": "Operation Rheinübung", "type": "Campaign", "url": "/campaigns/rheinubung" },
      { "id": "gunther-lutjens", "title": "Günther Lütjens", "type": "Personnel", "url": "/personnel/gunther-lutjens" },
      { "id": "tirpitz", "title": "Battleship Tirpitz", "type": "Armament", "url": "/armaments/tirpitz" }
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "tirpitz",
    "name": "Battleship Tirpitz",
    "type": "Battleship",
    "nation": "Germany",
    "class": "Bismarck-class",
    "image": "/storage/images/armaments/naval/tirpitz.jpg",
    "commissioned": "1941-02-25",
    "fate": "Sunk by RAF Tallboy bombs, 12 November 1944",
    "displacement_tonnes": 52600,
    "length_m": 251,
    "crew": 2608,
    "armament": {
      "main": "8× 38 cm SK C/34 guns",
      "secondary": ["12× 15 cm guns", "16× 10.5 cm guns"]
    },
    "speed_knots": 30.8,
    "summary": "The Tirpitz spent most of the war anchored in Norwegian fjords as a 'fleet in being', threatening Allied convoys to the Soviet Union without ever engaging in a major surface action. RAF Lancaster bombers of 617 and 9 Squadrons used 12,000-pound Tallboy bombs to finally sink her at Tromsø on 12 November 1944, with the loss of approximately 1,000 men.",
    "sources": [
      { "ref": "Woodward, David. The Tirpitz and the Battle for Norway. Norton, 1953." }
    ],
    "related_records": [
      { "id": "bismarck", "title": "Battleship Bismarck", "type": "Armament", "url": "/armaments/bismarck" },
      { "id": "atlantic", "title": "Battle of the Atlantic", "type": "Campaign", "url": "/campaigns/atlantic" },
      { "id": "v2-rocket", "title": "V-2 Rocket", "type": "Armament", "url": "/armaments/v2-rocket" }
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "type-viic-uboat",
    "name": "Type VIIC U-boat",
    "type": "Submarine",
    "nation": "Germany",
    "image": "/storage/images/armaments/naval/type-viic.jpg",
    "years_of_service": "1940–1945",
    "manufacturer": "Multiple yards",
    "crew": 44,
    "displacement_surfaced_tonnes": 769,
    "displacement_submerged_tonnes": 871,
    "length_m": 67.1,
    "max_speed_surfaced_knots": 17.7,
    "max_speed_submerged_knots": 7.6,
    "range_km": 14800,
    "armament": {
      "torpedoes": "14× G7a/G7e torpedoes (5 tubes: 4 bow, 1 stern)",
      "gun": "1× 88 mm deck gun"
    },
    "units_produced": 568,
    "summary": "The Type VIIC was the backbone of the U-boat campaign — the most produced German submarine of the war. These boats conducted the Battle of the Atlantic, sinking millions of tonnes of Allied shipping. Of the 568 Type VIIC boats built, 429 were lost — a casualty rate of over 75%. Their crews faced the highest fatality rate of any service in any country during the war.",
    "sources": [
      { "ref": "Kemp, Paul. U-Boats Destroyed: German Submarine Losses in the World Wars. Arms and Armour, 1997." },
      { "ref": "Rossler, Eberhard. The U-boat: The Evolution and Technical History of German Submarines. Arms and Armour, 1981." }
    ],
    "related_records": [
      { "id": "karl-donitz", "title": "Karl Dönitz", "type": "Personnel", "url": "/personnel/karl-donitz" },
      { "id": "otto-kretschmer", "title": "Otto Kretschmer", "type": "Personnel", "url": "/personnel/otto-kretschmer" },
      { "id": "wolfgang-luth", "title": "Wolfgang Lüth", "type": "Personnel", "url": "/personnel/wolfgang-luth" },
      { "id": "uboat-campaign", "title": "U-boat Campaign", "type": "Campaign", "url": "/campaigns/uboat-campaign" }
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "scharnhorst",
    "name": "Battlecruiser Scharnhorst",
    "type": "Battlecruiser",
    "nation": "Germany",
    "class": "Scharnhorst-class",
    "image": "/storage/images/armaments/naval/scharnhorst.jpg",
    "commissioned": "1939-01-07",
    "fate": "Sunk 26 December 1943 (Battle of North Cape)",
    "displacement_tonnes": 38100,
    "length_m": 234.9,
    "crew": 1968,
    "armament": {
      "main": "9× 28 cm SK C/34 guns (3 triple turrets)",
      "secondary": ["12× 15 cm guns", "14× 10.5 cm AA guns"]
    },
    "speed_knots": 31.5,
    "summary": "The Scharnhorst and her sister Gneisenau conducted the most successful German surface raider sortie of the war (Operation Berlin, 1941), sinking 22 Allied ships totalling 115,000 tonnes. She was sunk during the Battle of the North Cape on 26 December 1943 by HMS Duke of York and escorting destroyers while attacking convoy JW 55B, with the loss of all but 36 of her crew of 1,968.",
    "sources": [
      { "ref": "Haarr, Geirr. The Sinking of the Scharnhorst. Seaforth, 2013." }
    ],
    "related_records": [
      { "id": "bismarck", "title": "Battleship Bismarck", "type": "Armament", "url": "/armaments/bismarck" },
      { "id": "gneisenau", "title": "Battlecruiser Gneisenau", "type": "Armament", "url": "/armaments/gneisenau" },
      { "id": "atlantic", "title": "Battle of the Atlantic", "type": "Campaign", "url": "/campaigns/atlantic" }
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "gneisenau",
    "name": "Battlecruiser Gneisenau",
    "type": "Battlecruiser",
    "nation": "Germany",
    "class": "Scharnhorst-class",
    "image": "/storage/images/armaments/naval/gneisenau.jpg",
    "commissioned": "1938-05-21",
    "fate": "Scuttled 27 March 1945, Gdynia; wreck cleared 1951",
    "displacement_tonnes": 38900,
    "length_m": 235.4,
    "crew": 1669,
    "armament": {
      "main": "9× 28 cm SK C/34 guns (3 triple turrets)",
      "secondary": ["12× 15 cm guns", "14× 10.5 cm AA guns"]
    },
    "speed_knots": 32,
    "summary": "Sister ship to the Scharnhorst, the Gneisenau conducted Operation Berlin alongside her in 1941, accounting for 22 Allied ships. She was severely damaged by RAF bombing at Brest in 1942 during the 'Thousand Bomber' attacks and the famous Channel Dash. Struck so often by mines and torpedoes that her crew nicknamed her the 'cellar dweller', she was eventually decommissioned and her guns transferred to shore batteries. She was scuttled as a blockship at Gdynia in March 1945.",
    "sources": [
      { "ref": "Groner, Erich. German Warships 1815–1945. Naval Institute Press, 1990." },
      { "ref": "Bekker, Cajus. Hitler's Naval War. Macdonald, 1974." }
    ],
    "related_records": [
      { "id": "scharnhorst", "title": "Battlecruiser Scharnhorst", "type": "Armament", "url": "/armaments/scharnhorst" },
      { "id": "bismarck", "title": "Battleship Bismarck", "type": "Armament", "url": "/armaments/bismarck" },
      { "id": "atlantic", "title": "Battle of the Atlantic", "type": "Campaign", "url": "/campaigns/atlantic" },
      { "id": "erich-raeder", "title": "Erich Raeder", "type": "Personnel", "url": "/personnel/erich-raeder" }
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "admiral-graf-spee",
    "name": "Pocket Battleship Admiral Graf Spee",
    "type": "Heavy Cruiser / Panzerschiff",
    "nation": "Germany",
    "class": "Deutschland-class",
    "image": "/storage/images/armaments/naval/graf-spee.jpg",
    "commissioned": "1936-01-06",
    "fate": "Scuttled 17 December 1939, mouth of River Plate",
    "displacement_tonnes": 16020,
    "length_m": 186,
    "crew": 1150,
    "armament": {
      "main": "6× 28 cm SK C/28 guns (2 triple turrets)",
      "secondary": ["8× 15 cm guns", "6× 10.5 cm AA guns"]
    },
    "speed_knots": 28.5,
    "summary": "The Admiral Graf Spee was one of Germany's three 'pocket battleships' — vessels designed to outgun any cruiser and outrun any battleship within treaty limits. She sank nine Allied merchant ships totalling 50,000 tonnes during her commerce-raiding cruise in the South Atlantic and Indian Ocean before being brought to action at the Battle of the River Plate (13 December 1939) by three British cruisers. Damaged, she took refuge in Montevideo. Facing international pressure to leave and believing a superior British force waited outside, Captain Hans Langsdorff scuttled her on 17 December 1939 and shot himself three days later.",
    "sources": [
      { "type": "primary", "ref": "National Archives (UK). ADM 234/155: Battle of the River Plate — Official Despatches, December 1939." },
      { "ref": "Pope, Dudley. The Battle of the River Plate. Kimber, 1956.", "note": "The classic account of the hunt and scuttling." },
      { "ref": "Millington-Drake, Eugen. The Drama of Graf Spee and the Battle of the Plate: A Documentary Anthology. Peter Davies, 1964." }
    ],
    "related_records": [
      { "id": "river-plate", "title": "Battle of the River Plate", "type": "Campaign", "url": "/campaigns/river-plate" },
      { "id": "scharnhorst", "title": "Battlecruiser Scharnhorst", "type": "Armament", "url": "/armaments/scharnhorst" },
      { "id": "erich-raeder", "title": "Erich Raeder", "type": "Personnel", "url": "/personnel/erich-raeder" }
    ]
  }
]
naval\\italy.json:
{
  "category": "Naval Vessels — Italy (Regia Marina)",
  "vessels": [
    {
      "name": "Littorio (later Italia)",
      "type": "Battleship",
      "class": "Littorio class",
      "commissioned": 1940,
      "displacement_tons": 45236,
      "length_m": 237.8,
      "armament": "9× 381mm guns, 12× 152mm guns, extensive AA",
      "fate": "Survived war; scuttled at La Spezia 1948",
      "notes": "Italy's most powerful battleship. Damaged twice by British torpedoes at Taranto (1940) and by a torpedo-bomber attack (1941). Renamed Italia in 1943 after Mussolini's fall; surrendered to the Allies."
    },
    {
      "name": "Vittorio Veneto",
      "type": "Battleship",
      "class": "Littorio class",
      "commissioned": 1940,
      "displacement_tons": 45752,
      "armament": "9× 381mm guns",
      "fate": "Survived war; scrapped 1950",
      "notes": "Participated in the Battle of Cape Matapan (1941) where it was torpedoed and barely escaped. Surrendered at Malta in September 1943."
    },
    {
      "name": "Zara",
      "type": "Heavy Cruiser",
      "class": "Zara class",
      "commissioned": 1931,
      "displacement_tons": 14530,
      "armament": "8× 203mm guns",
      "fate": "Sunk 29 March 1941",
      "notes": "Sunk by British destroyers at the Battle of Cape Matapan after being disabled. The battle was a decisive Italian defeat — three heavy cruisers and two destroyers lost in a night action."
    },
    {
      "name": "Adua (submarine)",
      "type": "Submarine",
      "class": "Adua class (600-series)",
      "commissioned": 1936,
      "displacement_tons": 695,
      "armament": "6× 533mm torpedo tubes, 1× 100mm deck gun",
      "fate": "Sunk 15 November 1942",
      "notes": "Representative of Italy's large submarine fleet — over 100 boats at war's start. Italian submarines operated extensively in the Mediterranean and Atlantic; they sank over 500,000 GRT of Allied shipping."
    }
  ]
}
naval\\japan.json:
[
  {
    "id": "yamato",
    "name": "Battleship Yamato",
    "type": "Battleship",
    "nation": "Japan",
    "class": "Yamato-class",
    "commissioned": "1941-12-16",
    "fate": "Sunk 7 April 1945",
    "displacement_tonnes": 72810,
    "length_m": 263,
    "crew": 2767,
    "armament": { "main": "9× 46cm/45 Type 94 guns (3 triple turrets)", "secondary": ["12× 15.5cm guns (removed 1944)", "24× 127mm AA guns", "152× 25mm AA guns"] },
    "speed_knots": 27,
    "armor_mm": { "belt": 410, "deck": 200, "turret_face": 650 },
    "summary": "The Yamato was the largest and most powerfully armed battleship ever built, carrying the largest guns ever fitted to a warship. Yet she was largely ineffective in combat — the era of the battleship had given way to the aircraft carrier. She was sunk by American carrier aircraft during Operation Ten-Go on 7 April 1945, on a suicide mission to Okinawa with fuel for only a one-way trip."
  },
  {
    "id": "shokaku",
    "name": "Fleet Carrier Shōkaku",
    "type": "Fleet Carrier",
    "nation": "Japan",
    "class": "Shōkaku-class",
    "commissioned": "1941-08-08",
    "fate": "Sunk 19 June 1944 (Battle of the Philippine Sea)",
    "displacement_tonnes": 32105,
    "length_m": 257.5,
    "crew": 1660,
    "aircraft_capacity": 84,
    "speed_knots": 34.2,
    "summary": "One of Japan's finest fleet carriers and veteran of Pearl Harbor, the Coral Sea, and Santa Cruz. Shōkaku and her sister Zuikaku were the premier carrier strike force of the Imperial Japanese Navy. She was sunk by US submarine Cavalla during the Battle of the Philippine Sea ('The Marianas Turkey Shoot')."
  }
]
